title: Observing, Describing, Participating Checklist

pages.text:
85
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permis-
sion to photocopy this worksheet is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details). Purchasers may
download a larger version of this worksheet from www.guilford.com/dbt-worksheets.
Mindfulness Worksheet 4a   (Mindfulness Handouts 4–4c)
Observing, Describing, Participating Checklist
Due Date:             Name:                                    Week Starting:
Check off mindfulness skills that you use when you use them. You can check each skill up to four
times. If you practice a skill more than four times, extend your checks toward the edge of the page,
or use the back of the page if needed.
Practice observing: Check off an exercise each time you do one.



   1.	What you see:     Watch without following what you see.



   2.	Sounds:     Sounds around you,     pitch and sound of someone’s
voice,     music.



   3.	Smells around you:     Aroma of food,     soap,     air as you walk.



   4.	The taste of what you eat and the act of eating.



   5.	Urges to do something:     Urge-surf,     notice urge to avoid,
    notice where in body urge is.



   6.	Body sensations:     Body scan,     sensation of walking,     body touching
something.



   7.	Thoughts coming in and out of your mind:     Imagine your mind as a river,
    as a conveyor belt.



   8.	Your breath:     Movement of stomach,     sensations of air in and out nose.



   9.	By expanding awareness:     To your entire body,     to space around you,
    to hugging a tree.



 10.	By opening the mind:     To each sensation arising, not attaching, letting go of each.



 11.	 Other (describe):
Practice describing: Check off an exercise each time you do one.



 12.	What you see outside of your body.



 13.	Thoughts, feelings, and body sensations inside yourself.



 14.	Your breathing.



 15.	Other (describe):
Practice participating: Check off an exercise each time you do one.



 16.	Dance to music.



 17.	 Sing along with music you are listening to.



 18.	Sing in the shower.



 19.	Sing and dance while watching TV.



 20.	Jump out of bed and dance or sing before getting dressed.



 21.	Go to a church that sings and join in the singing.



 22.	Play karaoke with friends or at a karaoke club or bar.



 23.	Throw yourself into what another person is saying.



 24.	Go running, riding, skating, walking; become one with the activity.



 25.	Play a sport and throw yourself into playing.



 26.	Become the count of your breath, becoming only “one” when you count 1, becoming
only “two” when you count 2, and so on.



 27.	Become a word as you slowly say the word over and over and over.



 28.	Throw caution to the wind, and throw yourself into a social or work activity.



 29.	Other (describe):
List any and all wise things you did this week:

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Observing, Describing, Participating Checklist
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Mindfulness is allowed to be tiny, sensory-safe, and done from bed or wheelchair. Noticing without judging counts even when the day is messy.
tiny_steps: Find one neutral thing in the room.
Return to the next safe action.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use eyes-open grounding if closing eyes feels unsafe.
Use quiet, low-scent, low-touch options for MCAS, trauma, and sensory overload.
No posture requirement; lying down or supported sitting is valid.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not force stillness when it increases pain, panic, dysphoria, or trauma activation.
